data.title: Opportunities In The Insurance Sector (Part 3): W. R. Berkley's Baby Bonds
data.description:
Co-authored by Strategic Yield.

We continue the series with W. R. Berkley Corporation (NYSE:WRB) and its financial instruments.

Company Overview

W. R. Berkley Corporation is an established player in the insurance and reinsurance industry. As of the end of the second quarter of 2025, the company had assets of approximately $42.7 billion and a market capitalization of $27.7 billion. Founded in 1967 and headquartered in Greenwich, Connecticut, USA, WRB maintains a strong financial position. The company's common stock is trading at $73.11, as you can see in the picture below:

WRB is rated as an "investment grade," as you can see the ratings from the major credit agencies below:

Below, we can see the comparison between WRB and the SPDR S&P Insurance ETF (KIE):

3-year total return:

5-year total return:

10-year total return:

We can see WRB outperforms KIE in all cases, as well as in the comparison with its peers:

W. R. Berkley has a 35-year history of paying dividends, with the last 19 years of dividend increases. The 12-month total dividend is $1.36, which at the current share price yields 1.86%. The dividend history is presented below:

And here you can observe the dividend yield for the past 5 years:

The 10-year dividend growth rate is 9.49%.

Baby bonds

W. R. Berkley has four exchange-traded securities:

All of them are rated "investment grade" by the major credit agencies. S&P gives a credit rating "BBB-," Moody's "Baa2," and Fitch "BBB." And all are traded below par.

(WRB.PR.F) is trading at $21.94, with an annual coupon of 5.10% (quarterly payments of $0.31875, or $1.275 per year). It matures on 12/30/2059 (approximately 35 years to maturity) and has a yield to maturity of 6.11%. The call date is 12/30/2024, making it callable at any time. 12 million notes were issued at a par value of $25 on 12/03/2019.

(NYSE:WRB.PR.G) is trading at $18.79, with an annual coupon of 4.25% (quarterly payments of $0.265625, or $1.0625 per year). It matures on 09/30/2060 (about 35 years to maturity) and has a yield to maturity of 6.08%, and the call date is 09/30/2025 (about 0.03 years to call). 10 million $25 notes were issued on 09/16/2020.

(NYSE:WRB.PR.E) is priced $24.15, with an annual coupon of 5.70% (quarterly payments of $0.35625, or $1.425 per year). It matures on 03/30/2058 (about 33 years to maturity), and the call date was 03/30/2023, making it callable at any time. Its yield to maturity is 6.08%. 7 million notes were issued at a par value of $25 each on 03/20/2018.

At the time of writing, (NYSE:WRB.PR.H) is trading at $18.70, with an annual coupon of 4.13% (quarterly payments of $0.2578125, or $1.03125 annual amount). Maturity is 03/30/2061 (about 36 years to maturity), and the yield to maturity is 5.92%. The call date is 03/30/2026 (close to 6 months to call). 12 million $25 notes were issued on 02/03/2021.

Disclosure: For those wondering how we calculate the yields, we calculate the internal rate of return for a series of cash flows. We include an initial investment (or current price), periodic payments, and a final payment at maturity, which allows us to calculate XIRR.

OTC bonds

Below are the over-the-counter bonds of W. R. Berkley with these characteristics:

These bonds are rated Baa1 by Moody’s, one notch higher than baby bonds. However, baby bonds offer a higher yield.

Why are WRB’s baby bonds attractive?

All four of WRB’s baby bonds are investment grade, with ratings of BBB- by S&P, Baa2 by Moody’s, and BBB by Fitch, making them among the safest in the insurance sector. Although their yields to maturity are not the highest, they offer the potential for capital gains as they trade below par. Unlike some higher-yielding but lower-rated baby bonds in the insurance sector, WRB’s bonds offer a level of security that is particularly valuable to investors seeking a balance between yield and safety.

Three of the bonds are already callable. This introduces a call risk, whereby WRB could redeem the bonds at par if market interest rates fall. However, at current prices below par, this risk is mitigated by the potential for capital gains.

Conclusion

W. R. Berkley Corporation has a solid financial profile, $42.7 billion in assets, and a strong credit rating. Its baby bonds offer yields to maturity of 5.92–6.11%, trade below par, and are investment grade, making them a safe choice for investors.

data.title: Trouble In Paradise: Long-Term Rates Are Rising
data.description:
The risk-management Fed cut

The Fed decreased the Federal Funds rate by 25 bps at the September meeting and signaled 2 more cuts in 2025, in October and December, and one more cut in 2026, down to 3.4%.

Fed Chair Powell said at the post-meeting press conference that this was a risk-management cut, and that the direction is towards the lower interest rates.

Essentially, the Fed is moving the Federal Funds rate from the restrictive level towards the neutral level.

The reason behind the cut is that the labor market is weakening. In the Fed's view, the labor supply is decreasing at the same time as the demand for labor is also decreasing, which is a risky situation, where the unemployment rate could spike if the layoffs accelerate. So, the normalization policy is designed as the pre-emptive risk-management policy - to prevent a recession.

There are three problems with the Fed's view and the current risk-management normalization policy:

  1. There is uncertainty where the neutral rate is, and it's possible that the neutral rate is at or even above 4%, given the economic resilience over the last year. Thus, the current policy rate could be already at a neutral level or even be stimulative. If that's the case, inflation could spike as the economy overheats.
  2. The unemployment rate is still at a full-employment level, and trying to stimulate the economy at a full employment is also inflationary, especially when the labor supply is decreasing, due to immigration policy and demographics.
  3. The risk-management normalization is happening with the core CPI approaching 3%, well above the 2% target. The Fed's view is that tariff-related inflation will be temporary, which allows the normalization policy - and basically to ignore inflation. That might be true, but if the Fed cuts boost wages, inflation could become more persistent and require the Fed to start hiking interest rates. That would be a Fed error.

The "Goldilocks paradise"

The Fed paints the Goldilocks paradise. In their view, the unemployment rate will stay below 4.5% indefinitely, and yet inflation will keep falling, down to 2.6% in 2026, all while the Federal Funds rate keeps dropping and stimulating economy, which is expected to grow at a perfect 1.8%, not too hot or too cold. That's goldilocks.

Obviously, this situation would be ideal for the stock market, and the bubble could continue to inflate, indefinitely. Yet, the S&P 500 (SP500) is trading at a Shiller PE ratio of 40, which is just below the 2000 dot-com level, but the Goldilocks situation could break the 2000 record valuation, and another 20% of growth in the S&P 500 puts the Shiller PE at 50! That's basically what the bulls are betting on - a Shiller PE ratio of 50.

The long-term rates problem

Thus, at this point, the Fed is stimulating the economy, and fiscal policy is stimulating the economy with a 6%+ deficit. What could go wrong?

There are only two things that are more difficult to control by the Fed and the Administration: 1) 10Y Treasury bond yield (US10Y), and 2) US Dollar value (DXY).

The 10Y Treasury Bond yield is the sum of Real interest rate and Breakeven inflation expectations. The key here is the Breakeven inflation expectations, which is a proxy for anchored inflation expectations, and this number cannot persistently exceed the 2.5% level.

If the market senses that the Fed is abandoning the 2% inflation target, the Breakeven inflation expectations would increase above 2.5% towards the 3% level, and above. This would signal that long-term inflation expectations are de-anchoring.

The Fed and the Administration could try to control the nominal 10Y yield via QE or other forms of yield curve control, but this would push the Real yield lower, possibly below 0%, while inflation expectations would be still de-anchored. In this situation, the US Dollar could collapse.

Without the yield curve control, the nominal yields would sharply rise and with it, the mortgage rates, which would burst the housing bubble, and the economy.

So, either the US Dollar collapses with the yield curve control, which would likely lead to the foreign capital outflows from the US assets and consequently burst the bubbles, or the long-term rates increase and crash the economy.

The reaction to the Fed cut

The 10-year yields fell below 4% right after the Fed cut, and quickly reversed and increased to over 4.13%.

The increase in 10Y yield was supported by falling initial claims for unemployment on Thursday. This tells you, if the labor market does not deteriorate, inflation could pick up, as the Fed cuts to stimulate a full employment level.

The 10Y yields are currently at a very important level. The long-term chart has the 4% yield right at the key uptrend support level (green line in the chart below). The break below the 4% level would signal a reversal in the uptrend and lower long-term yields - and this would be consistent with a slowing economy and possibly a recession.

But the Fed is trying to prevent a recession, and that points to higher 10Y yields. If the 4% support level holds, the 10Y yield is likely to retest the 5% top fairly soon and possibly break above 5% as the uptrend continues.

Most importantly, the 10Y breakeven inflation is right below the 2.5% level, and it's been there over the last 3 years, as the chart below shows. Once the 2.5% level is persistently broken, the Fed will have to panic, like in 2022.

At that point, the Fed will have to turn hawkish and start signaling an interest rate hike as the next move (like in 2022) or allow de-anchoring of long-term inflation expectations and essentially abandon the 2% inflation target.

The key will be the labor market. If the labor market improves, inflation will become more persistent, and the Fed will have to act. But now consider the fact that the Fed will have a new Chair next year and consider the issue of Fed independence.

Implications

The Goldilocks paradise is a utopia. It's impossible to achieve it in the current environment. Yes, the short-term rates are managed by the Fed, but the long-term rates are more difficult to manage, and the real challenge is managing the value of the US Dollar.

The stock market is a bubble with the Shiller PE at 40, but the bulls expect it to rise to 50 if they expect an additional 20% increase from here (Shiller PE is adjusted for inflation). The increase in long-term rates or a collapsing USD could burst the bubble.

Gold (GLD) is the only asset that reflects the risk of de-anchoring of inflation expectations or a collapsing US Dollar, along with other risks like geopolitics. That's a warning, as the chart below shows.

data.title: Annaly Capital: Yield Curve Could Normalize (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
NLY stock: yield curve flattened

I last analyzed Annaly Capital Management, Inc. (NYSE:NLY) stock on July 9th with an article titled "Annaly Vs. Rithm Capital: If You Really Want mREIT, Hold Rithm.” The analysis rated NLY as a Sell after analyzing the impacts of an inverted yield curve and the delinquency rates on the mREIT sector. Since then, two material developments have motivated this examination: the company’s earnings report (ER) released on 7.23 for its FQ2 and also the changes in interest rates. These developments have led me to see large odds for the yield inversion curve to end, which in turn led to an upgrade of NLY’s rating to hold.

Let me start with changes in the interest rates. As seen in the chart below, the 3-Month treasury rates have been hovering above the 10-Year Treasury rates most of the time since early 2023, resulting in an inverted yield curve. Since my last writing, both rates have declined and they are now nearly equalized. To wit, after the Fed’s rate cut decision announced on 9.17, both the 3-Month and 10-Year Treasury rates have settled at 4.04%, resulting in a relatively flat yield curve.

Next, I will detail why lower rates and a flattened curve could help NLY.

NLY stock: benefits from the flattened yield curve already started

The spread between short- and long-term interest rates is worth close monitoring for mREIT stocks because the spread is a key profit driver for their business in my model. The thinking in this model is detailed in my earlier article. The most relevant part is quoted below for ease of reference.

… mREIT stocks in general (and NLY to a large degree) make money on the spread between long-term and short-term rates. The thicker the long-short spread, the easier it is for mREITs to make money. The business model of mREITs is to primarily invest in mortgage-backed securities (“MBS”), which offer fixed interest rates based on the prevailing rates at the time of issuance. Wider spreads mean their longer-term MBS holdings generally offer higher interest rates compared to the shorter-term borrowings they use to finance these investments.

If you subscribe to the above thinking, you can see why a flattened yield curve is a net positive in my view for NLY. Further, judging by the financial results reported in its FQ2 ER, I believe the benefits from a flattened yield curve have already started to materialize. As seen from the screenshot below (taken from its ER), the company reported improved profitability measured by a range of metrics.

For example, its average yield on interest-earning assets improved to 5.42% in the quarter, vs. 5.18% in the previous quarter and 5.17% a year ago. In the meantime, the average GAAP cost of interest-bearing liabilities decreased to 4.76% for the quarter, vs. 4.77% in the previous quarter and 5.43% a year ago. As a result, net interest spread widened to 0.66%, vs. 0.41% in the previous quarter and -0.26% a year ago.

NLY stock: leverage in focus

Besides improving profitability metrics, lower interest rates are also beneficial for NLY given its considerable degree of leverage. As seen from the chart above, its leverage slightly increased to 5.8x in the quarter in non-GAAP terms, vs. 5.7x for the last quarter and 5.8x for the last year. In GAAP terms, the leverage is even higher at 7.1x. To provide a broader context, the chart below provides its debt-to-equity ratio over the past decade. As seen, the average debt-to-equity ratio over the entire period is about 6.168x in GAAP terms, and the current ratio is notably above the long-term average. With an above-average leverage ratio, the company’s borrowing costs and also valuation multiples could respond more sensitively to future rate cuts, which is what the prevailing expectation in the market.

NLY stock: valuation

Speaking of valuation, I see a bit of ambiguity here. In terms of P/E ratios, NLY is currently trading at a large discount compared to its peers. As detailed in the next table, the non-GAAP TTM P/E for Annaly is about 7.74x only currently, which is more than 35% below the sector median of 12.06. The FWD P/E of 7.55 represents a similar 33% discount from the sector median of 11.26.

On the other hand, NLY’s price-to-book value ratio (P/BV) paints a different picture as you can see from the following chart. In the long term, Annaly's P/BV ratio value has generally fluctuated closely around its average of 0.984 as seen. The ratio remained relatively stable, within a range of 0.90x to 1.1x, most of the time except for a few wide swings. This makes good sense to me intuitively as I don’t see any fundamental reasons for the P/BV ratios of an mREIT company to stray too far from 1x. But currently, the ratio is 1.177, which is considerably above the long-term average.

Other risks and final thoughts

A possible justification for its premium P/BV ratio is the outlook for continued rate cuts, which could boost the growth of its book value and better support its valuation multiple. Also, an analysis of an mREIT stock won’t be complete without taking a look at its dividend yield. NLY currently yields about 12.4% as you can see from the chart below. This is slightly above its long-term average of 11.9%, indicating a neutral return/risk ratio. Although note that the stock has suffered dividend cuts recently and its yield has varied in a wide range between about 0% and as high as 24%, accentuating its sensitivity to the interest rate cycles.

For investors who can look past these near to mid-term issues, I feel neutral about NLY's long-term prospects. This is considering the growth potential of its residential credit, MSR portfolios, and also book value, assuming a normalized yield curve ahead.

To close, this NLY reexamination is mainly motivated ty two new catalysts: its FQ2 ER and also the changes in interest rates. In particular, the short- and long-term rates are now nearly equalized after the latest Fed’s rate cut decision. I see large odds for the yield curve to further steepen, given the outlook of further cuts. These developments have shifted its return/risk ratio materially in my model and resulted in a rating adjustment to Hold.

data.title: Nintendo: Sales Are Booming, But Where Are The Profits?
data.description:
Introduction

In a previous article on Nintendo (OTCPK:NTDOY), I pointed out the strong start of Switch 2 from its early days; in fact, it has broken every record. However, even though that was a positive catalyst, I still had doubts about a potential buy rating. My concern was based on two aspects:

  • The first was that I was skeptical about Switch 2 future sales.
  • The second concerned tariff risk, as management had declared that it wouldn't pass on the higher costs to consumers. That means lower profit margins.

Regarding the first one, in hindsight, my worries were excessive. In the first 4 days, 3.50 million units were sold; after 7 weeks following the release, over 6 million units were sold. A pretty good result overall, so why do I still consider Nintendo a hold?

The reason lies in my second doubt, namely the tariffs. As I expected, they are weighing on profit margin, but this is not the only component that is sinking the profitability. As I'll show you, even though the Switch 2 costs $450, its price is not sufficient to generate a profit.

Sales are booming, but it's still not enough

In terms of sales, the Switch 2 was a worldwide success: the console had the best start in history. Nintendo supporters were eagerly waiting for an upgrade; after all, the first Switch was released 8 years ago. Switch 2 success was so big that even management was caught off guard; that's why the current demand exceeds the supply. If the company had been properly prepared regarding its supply chain, the potential sales could have been even higher.

Consider that Switch and Switch 2 active players are currently 128 million, but only 6 million belong to the latter. This means that potentially, there are hundreds of millions of users left who can upgrade to the Switch 2. By the way, the Switch is not dead yet, as you can see in the chart above. There is still some momentum to exploit before the users all get caught up by the newest console.

Given such results, the net sales could do nothing but explode. Compared to the year, in Q1 FY2026 (the first one that includes Switch 2 sales), net sales increased by 132%. The dedicated video game platform segment, namely consoles and video games, experienced an even higher increase, +142%, compared to the previous year.

Clearly, the majority of this improvement was attributable to Switch 2 sales. When it comes to software, Switch keeps selling more than Switch 2, but that is quite normal. The former has a much wider user base, and the new console presents backward compatibility. For those who don't want to buy the Switch 2 right away, it still makes sense to buy a game for the previous console, as it can also be played on the new one.

Overall, Nintendo's net sales are skyrocketing, so why is the stock not a buy? The answer lies in the profitability collapse.

As you can observe, the operating margin declined to 9.90% from 22.10%. This data was influenced by the rising advertising cost (+115%) and R&D (+12%), but these two factors only explain a small part of the whole story. The real problem is about gross profit margin.

Net sales soared by 132%, but gross profit went up by just 21%. Take a look at the gross profit margin: from 61.80% to 32.20%, almost cut in half. What happened?

Switch 2 is not as profitable as Switch

The trigger of the gross profit margin drop was a higher proportion of hardware sales over total sales. In a sense, all this was easily predictable, but maybe not its magnitude. Companies that sell consoles know from the beginning that the profit lies mostly in selling software (mainly video games), not the console itself. That's because software has higher profitability thanks to its scalability.

Therefore, when Switch 2 was released, a drop in profitability was widely expected, as well as a gradual recovery once the software starts to weigh more. Nintendo's problem is that Switch 2 is less profitable than Switch, as stated by the management itself:

The gross profit margin declined by 29.5 points to 32.3%, reflecting the fact that proportion of hardware sales became higher with the launch of Nintendo Switch 2, and Nintendo Switch 2, which has a lower profit margin than Nintendo Switch, accounted for a higher proportion of those hardware sales.

Overall, not an ideal situation given the recent tariffs implementation, but my impression is that it is more serious than it seems. Here is why.

These are the results for the H1 FY2018, so it includes the first 6 months since the Switch was launched. In this case net sales climbed as well, but the gross profit margin didn't go through a 45% drop. The gross profit margin reached 38.40%, a higher level than the current 32.30%.

For the whole FY2018, the gross profit margin remained 38% and then rose to 42% the following year.

However, at that point, the Switch had already been out for two years, and around 34 million units had been sold. As a result, I assume that we can identify 20-25 million as the figure at which the reduction in gross profit margin due to the sale of the new console is more than offset by software sales. Today, Switch 2 is still too distant from getting 20 million sales, and it is even less profitable than its predecessor.

On this last aspect, I think it is worth dwelling on since it has a crucial importance in the whole reasoning. How much is the Switch 2 less profitable? The management didn't give us a hint, but we can make some hypotheses to better understand the whole picture. At first glance it might seem that Switch 2 is more profitable than Switch; after all, the first one costs $450 and the second one just $300; it is actually the opposite.

Let's start with the main feature: Switch 2 is much more powerful than Switch, and this means a substantially higher production cost. We don't have official estimates, but analysts believe that just the materials cost $400, and we are excluding from the equation logistic and advertising costs. Furthermore, something that management couldn't predict Nintendo is facing double-digit tariffs if it wants to sell in the US, and that makes the console even more unprofitable. The more it sells, the more money it loses, at least until users compensate for the losses by purchasing new games.

Finally, if we include in the equation also the inflation factor, Switch appears to be much more profitable than Switch 2. When launched, it cost $300, namely about $400 in today's money, just $50 less than Switch 2. Yet, production costs borne by the latter are way higher. Just the difference in processors between the two consoles is $50-$70, and there would be other costs to factor in, such as the cost of a larger display and tariffs. All in all, the Switch 2 risks being a black hole for Nintendo's profits in the coming quarters.

Conclusion

Switch 2 was a success in terms of units sold, but for each one of them, Nintendo's profitability worsens. Nobody expected an improvement in this phase, but I think the drop was too marked. In particular, US tariffs are exacerbating the issue, and management could not have predicted this when it released the Switch 2. In that respect, no one is to blame: the company was simply unlucky to release the new console (after eight years) during the tariff period.

We will see how the situation develops, but I believe that gross profit is likely to fall below 30% in the coming quarters. When new software starts to sell more (more players are needed), gross profit will increase once again, but that moment may still be distant.

Since price multiples are quite high and I don't think they will reach 2017 levels, buying Nintendo today might seem a risky move compared to the potential benefits. Anyway, I don't consider it as a sell; it might surprise me, as happened in the past.

In the event that the Switch 2 sells much more than the forecasts, the company could have already bottomed out its gross profit margin within a year. Estimates for March 2026 are 15 million units sold: if they get to 20 million or 25 million, the current price multiples might be justified. With Nintendo everything is possible; after all, even management did not expect such a speedy start for the Switch 2.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Village Super Market Is Super Cheap
data.description:
Whenever I rate a company a "Buy," I'm making the claim that shares should outperform the broader market for the foreseeable future. Lately, that has not been the case with Village Super Market (NASDAQ:VLGEA). Since I reaffirmed the company as a "Buy" candidate back in early April of this year, shares are up only 11.1% at a time when the S&P 500 is up 23.5%. Despite this underperformance, however, since I began writing about it regularly in December 2021, in an article in which I rated it a "Buy," the stock is up 100.9%. That's comfortably above the 45.4% increase that the broader market experienced.

Although this recent underperformance is disappointing, management continues to grow the business. Even though the number of locations remains flat, comparable store sales continue to grow. On top of this, while the company’s asset quality is certainly lower than other similar enterprises, its share price is also considerably lower than most of these firms. This suggests that while the company likely does deserve to trade at a discount to the competition, the size of the discount it's trading for now is probably unjustified.

Still on sale

The most recent fundamental data that we have regarding Village Super Market covers through the third quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. This chain of supermarkets, which falls under its ShopRite, Fairway, and Gourmet Garage brand names, is the second-largest member of Wakefern Food Corporation, which itself operates as the largest retailer-owned food cooperative and owner in the nation. This is a highly competitive space, but one in which the company has continued to achieve growth.

Take the most recent quarter as an example. During that time, revenue for the company came in at $563.7 million. That's 3.2% above the $546.4 million that the company reported one year earlier. This rise in sales was driven in part by a 1.9% increase in same-store sales. But it was also driven by two replacement stores that management commissioned. You see, management has been keeping its location count essentially flat from year to year. But it has been focusing on growing its physical footprint in another way.

In early April of this year, the company opened a 72,000-square-foot ShopRite store out of New Jersey that replaced what was then an existing 44,000-square-foot store. And a little more than a year before that, in March 2024, it opened an 83,000-square-foot replacement ShopRite location in New Jersey as well, replacing a 32,000-square foot store. This decision to close down smaller stores to focus on larger ones has proven to be beneficial, clearly driving a good portion of the firm's revenue growth over this time.

With revenue rising, profitability also improved. Net income, for instance, grew from $9 million in the third quarter of 2024 to $11.2 million at the same time this year. On an adjusted basis, however, the increase was from $9.6 million to $11.6 million. The company had to adjust for certain items, primarily pre-opening costs for the first stores. Other profitability metrics for the business also grew. Operating cash flow, for instance, expanded from $9.3 million to $10.5 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a jump from $11.9 million to $20.4 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $18.8 million to $23.1 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first nine months of the 2025 fiscal year compared to the same time in 2024. Revenue grew from $1.66 billion to $1.72 billion. That's an increase of 3.8% year over year. As I mentioned already, the firm's decision to essentially upgrade to larger-formatted stores certainly helped here. But another contributor ended up being a rise in same-store sales of 2.2%, which matched the 2.2% achieved at the same time the previous year.

The rise in revenue for the company brought with it higher profits as well. Net income popped from $35 million to $40.9 million, while adjusted net income grew from $35.7 million to $41.3 million. Operating cash flow expanded nicely from $58 million to $71.5 million. And the adjusted operating cash flow figure for the company also grew, climbing from $65 million to $70.1 million. Finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $69.3 million to $79.3 million.

When it comes to the rest of this year, there is uncertainty about what the future holds. Obviously, I am concerned about broader economic conditions. I firmly believe that the United States is heading toward a recession. And even though people need to eat, it's not unthinkable that food could be impacted. The fact of the matter is that, in the most recent quarter, 95.7% of the company's revenue came from things like grocery products, dairy products, frozen foods, health and beauty care products, liquor, produce, meat, deli, seafood, bakery products, prepared foods, and floral offerings.

Historically, food away from home spending has increased over time. And that trend will probably continue in the long run. However, it is also true that because of the higher costs involved, consumers are currently cutting back and focusing more on food at home. But according to recent data, somewhere between 60% and 70% of Americans are concerned about food prices. According to one source, which looked at the start of 2025, traditional supermarkets are even experiencing a decline in traffic, with consumers shifting toward lower priced items as well. I'm not expecting a huge sales decline, if any at all. But we could see some weakness on that front if economic conditions continue to worsen.

If we assume that the rest of this year will look like the first nine months of the year did on a year-over-year basis, we would expect net profits to come in at around $59 million. Adjusted operating cash flow would total around $99.4 million, while EBITDA would be $111.7 million. If we use these estimates, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. Especially on a forward basis, the stock does look rather cheap to me. It's also cheap compared to other similar firms. In the table below, I stacked it up against five similar enterprises. And what I found was that, on both a price-to-earnings basis and on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, our prospect ended up being the cheapest of the group. And only one of the companies was cheaper than it is on a price-to-operating cash flow basis.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Village Super Market 9.4 5.6 4.7
Natural Grocers by Vitamin Cottage (NGVC) 19.3 13.1 12.2
The Kroger Co. (KR) 18.3 8.5 7.5
Sprouts Farmers Market (SFM) 27.6 18.0 15.7
Casey's General Stores (CASY) 34.6 17.1 17.7
Albertsons Companies (ACI) 11.3 4.4 5.5

Another way to value the company would be through the lens of the price-to-book multiple. At present, the company is trading at just a slight premium to book value, with a price-to-book multiple of 1.16. For context, the other players that I compared it to are trading at multiples, as demonstrated in the chart below, of between 3.22 and 9.66. So even if Village Super Market were to trade at the lowest of these other players, it would mean an upside of 177.6% for shareholders.

This is not to say that some discount is unwarranted. One of the things I don't like about the company is that asset quality is lower than I would like it to be. In the two charts below, you can see its return on equity followed by its return on assets in the subsequent chart, both relative to its five competitors. While it is more competitive using the return on assets approach, it is the weakest when it comes to return on equity. This certainly justifies some discount. But it's difficult to imagine it justifies a discount as large as what we are seeing.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I think the market continues to undervalue Village Super Market. I don't expect the company to double or triple. I don't even expect it to rise by 70%. But I do think it deserves to trade higher than it is today. Because of this, I have no problem keeping it rated a solid "Buy" for now.

data.title: Gladstone Capital: One Of The Few Buying Opportunities In The BDC Segment
data.description:
Gladstone Capital (NASDAQ:GLAD) has been one of my first BDCs that I have covered here on the platform, assigning a buy rating. My original thesis was issued in December 2023, where I indicated a clear buy due to a combination of almost 10% yield and fundamentals that were strong enough to support the income over the foreseeable future.

After holding a long position in GLAD for about a year, I decided to take profits home and liquidate the position. At that time, I had realized ~40% in total return, while the overall BDC market (BIZD) had returned roughly 13%. My motivation for doing that was simple—i.e., GLAD at P/NAV of 1.31x seemed overvalued relative to the incremental earnings prospects and how other similar peers were priced.

Timing-wise, it could not be a better decision. See the chart below:

After exiting GLAD, the returns have been unpleasant, to say the least.

As a result of these declines, the P/NAV has gone down accordingly, albeit still revolving around 1.1x. This kind of premium continues to imply that there is something special about this BDC. Namely, while GLAD trades at ~10% premium, the sector median has a ~10% discount.

Let me revisit my thesis on GLAD now to see whether, in my opinion, it could be worth getting GLAD slowly but surely back in our BDC portfolios.

Thesis review

I will start with two, arguably, most critical aspects that we have to focus on when assessing BDC NAV and yield sustainability: net investment income per share generation and leverage.

In Q2, 2025, the NII per share result for GLAD landed at $0.50, which was almost exactly in line with the figures achieved in the three prior quarters, which had already included the consequences from the Fed's interest rate cuts in 2024. Before that—in Q2, 2024—GLAD was generating NII per share of $0.57, which in relation to the current base dividend was actually a fully acceptable level.

The thing is that the current base dividend coverage for GLAD stands at 101%, which is obviously an indication of a non-existent margin of safety. Granted, GLAD is not unique here as a large share of BDCs is exposed to the same issue—e.g., sector average base dividend coverage is just 102%. Yet, what is a bit different is that most BDCs that are also operating on thin ice have their yields above 10%. For GLAD, we are talking about 8.5%, which means if there is a cut, the post-cut dividend yield would indeed shrink to something totally unacceptable (given that it is a BDC).

So far, the story does not sound that great.

Fortunately, things are not as bad as they might seem. I will give you the three most important reasons.

First, GLAD has the absolute lowest leverage in the entire BDC space. The debt-to-equity is 0.59x, while the overall sector average is 1.19x. And even with this kind of overcapitalization, GLAD can cover the base dividend. Barely, but still covers. There is tangible evidence that going forward we should see GLAD optimizing the capital structure to something more reasonable and shareholder (earnings) friendly. On this one, I have dedicated the next point.

Second, as the base rates have declined and the cost of financing has gone down accordingly, it has created a more favorable ground for increased M&A activity. For BDCs, this is great news, and especially for those that have spare financial capacity to capitalize on that market. The idea is simple: the larger the portfolio, the larger the income base from which to capture spreads, which, in turn, feed into the NII per share generation in an accretive manner.

By listening to the most recent earnings conference call, it is encouraging to hear confirmation from Robert L. Marcotte - President of GLAD:

The vast majority of the anticipated portfolio events are behind us. And between our remaining new deal pipeline and new investment opportunities, we anticipate a resurgence in our portfolio growth.

Just to close the topic of interest rate-induced positives, it is also worth mentioning the fact that GLAD carries about 10% of its portfolio in pure-play and cash-generating equity positions. Here we have to factor in greater probabilities for GLAD in monetizing part of these stakes, which usually result in significant realized gains. Many BDCs do not have this luxury, most have it under 10% and those that have typically have secured it through restructuring of underperforming loans.

Third, GLAD is very similar to Gladstone Investment (GAIN), Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN) and Fidus Investment (FDUS) - all of which trade at premiums, carry high-quality equity stakes, have conservative capital structures, and play in the lower middle market segment. Speaking of the LMM, the focus is really put on companies with EBITDA of $3-25 million, which is where large institutional giants do not directly compete, as it simply does not make sense for them to invest their resources to access returns that do not move the needle in the context of their multi-billion portfolios. As a result of this, GLAD, just as the aforementioned peers, can enjoy above-average spreads and, importantly, avoid significant pressures from the BDC sector-wide spread compression. For example, based on Q2 2025 earnings results we can see that GLAD has been able to secure spreads of ~700 basis points, while those that operate in the middle and/or upper market space - Morgan Stanley Direct Lending Fund (MSDL) and Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL) - are really transacting in the area of 450 to 550 basis points.

The bottom line

Very recently, I issued an article on GAIN, which has a similar equity story - i.e., slight premium over NAV, ~100% dividend coverage, below average leverage, and LMM exposure. As the title suggests - Gladstone Investment: Most BDCs Will Cut, But Not This Monthly Yielder - my stance is bullish on GAIN.

In GLAD's case, there are two important differences:

1. As opposed to GAIN, the equity exposure is 10% (not 37%). Given that GAIN also has a covered dividend and the P/NAN metrics are almost identical, I would say that GAIN has a slight advantage here (i.e., higher probabilities of accessing abnormal gains from equity monetization).

2. GLAD has a debt-to-equity of 0.59x, while GAIN has it at 1.06x. In this case, GLAD has a clear advantage in growing the portfolio in a prudent fashion (which is inherently an NII per share accretive thing).

All in all, in my view, it is time to reopen a position in GLAD.

data.title: IHI: Medical Device ETF Continues To Lag, No Momentum In Sight
data.description:
Healthcare stocks continue to lag relative to the S&P 500. I pulled up a total return chart of the iShares U.S. Medical Devices ETF (NYSEARCA:IHI) versus the SPDR® S&P 500® ETF (SPY). This segment of the Healthcare sector is now at its worst level compared to SPY going back to 2012.

It had been a reliable alpha generator through early 2020, but that all reversed during and after the pandemic. I was neutral on IHI back in August 2024, and I reiterate a "Hold" rating today. Unfortunately, the valuation remains lofty, while the chart is stuck in neutral during what has been a raging bull market for the broader global stock market. IHI has returned just 4% since my summer 2024 analysis.

IHI vs. SPY: Fresh Multi-Year Relative Lows

According to the issuer, IHI offers investors "exposure to U.S. companies that manufacture and distribute medical devices." The ETF gives "targeted access to domestic medical device stocks" and is used by investors to express an industry view within the Healthcare sector. IHI seeks to track the investment results of an index composed of U.S. equities in the medical devices industry.

IHI is a medium-sized ETF with $4.2 billion in assets under management as of Sept. 17, 2025. That’s down from $5 billion at the time of my previous assessment 13 months ago. The annual expense ratio is moderate at 38 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is very low at just 43 basis points.

Share-price momentum has been particularly lousy in recent months, resulting in a poor F ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. IHI is also somewhat risky, largely due to its concentrated portfolio and above-market volatility readings. On liquidity, the fund appears more favorable. Its average daily volume exceeds 600,000 shares, while the median 30-day bid/ask spread is tight at only two basis points, per iShares.

For an update on the portfolio, the 4-star, "Neutral"-rated ETF by Morningstar has mixed exposure across the style box. Almost half of the fund is classified as large cap, while the allocation ever so slightly edges toward growth over value. To be clear, IHI is a 100% US equity product, so you don’t get any international diversification by owning it.

On valuation, the price-to-earnings ratio is at a premium to the broader stock market, above 25x. Pair that lofty multiple with a long-term earnings growth rate of just 9.6%, and the resulting PEG ratio is high, closer to three than two.

IHI: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

The 100% Healthcare Sector Fund is quite concentrated. More than three-quarters of the portfolio is made up of its 10 biggest positions. The top three stocks account for about 40% of IHI.

So, monitoring both the fundamentals and technicals of Abbott Laboratories (ABT), Intuitive Surgical, Inc. (ISRG), and Boston Scientific Corporation (BSX) is key. Income investors will also likely shy away from IHI due to its consistently low dividend yield.

IHI: Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally, September is the worst month when scanning price returns over the past 10 years. The trend improves in October and peaks in November. So, we could actually see the fund begin to trend higher if history is a guide.

IHI: Calendar Trends Soon To Turn Positive

The Technical Take

With a mid-20s P/E and a focused portfolio, IHI’s technical situation is mixed. Notice in the chart below that shares have consolidated in a trading range between $59 and $63. So long as that area persists, it’s difficult to be all that bullish or bearish. Moreover, the long-term 200-day moving average has turned flat in its slope, suggesting that there’s an ongoing battle between the longs and shorts.

Also, take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. It has printed a series of lower highs as IHI has bounced around. That’s a mildly bearish sign. The good news is that with a high amount of volume by price down to $50, there should be ample cushion if we see a downside breakdown. A rally through $63 would make me bullish for a retest of the 2021 peak of $67.

IHI: Weakening RSI, Tight Recent Trading Range, Flat 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a "Hold" rating on IHI. The fund has underperformed for years at this point, and the valuation does not scream bargain on a growth-adjusted basis. The chart, meanwhile, reaffirms the likelihood that more sideways price action is on tap.

data.title: Embraer: Defense Momentum Builds, But Labor Strike Clouds Execution
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The history of Embraer S.A. (NYSE:ERJ) remains one of the most interesting in the Latin American industrial universe. We are talking about a company that, far from competing in the league of giants like Boeing (BA) or Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF), found its own space in a very defined niche: regional jets with less than 150 seats. From there, ERJ built an identity that today is complemented by businesses in executives, defense and services, achieving a diversification uncommon in the sector.

Analyzing the results, we see that Q2 2025 confirmed this structural potential, with revenues of $1.8B (+22% YoY), a backlog record of $29.7B (+40% YoY), and operating margins that are starting to show signs of solidity after several years of volatility. However, the quarter also showed us that execution risks are still present: ex-Eve adjusted FCF was negative again at $161.6M, mainly due to working capital in preparation for more deliveries in H2 2025.

In addition, it is important to note that the external context also provides nuances. Fitch revised Embraer’s credit outlook to Positive, with a BBB-rating, highlighting that:

Embraer’s operating performance is likely to improve as deliveries rebound, driven by scale gains, prior cost-structure improvements, a better product mix, and expansion of its profitable services business.

However, not everything is good news, since at the same time, in Brazil an indefinite strike was unleashed that threatens to affect the pace of production just when ERJ needs to show conversion backlog into cash.

This is why, when looking at the information as a whole, my current position is clear: although the structural history is solid and attractive, I consider that the short term requires us to be prudent.

Overview

The H1 2025 showed a strengthened Embraer in all its lines of business. From the point of view of its backlog, we see that it reached $29.7B, the largest in its history, distributed between Commercial ($13.1B, with a maximum in eight years), Defense ($4.3B; double than one year ago), Services ($4.9B; record level), and Executives ($7.4B).

Also, the 61 deliveries for the quarter (19 commercial, 38 executive, and 4 defense) reflect a growth of 30% YoY, with particular dynamism in executive jets (+64% YoY).

However, I think that in addition to looking at recent results, it is important to look ahead, and here I consider that the most relevant point of the last weeks came from defense, where ERJ begins to advance slowly but steadily. In this line, Portugal extended its contract with Embraer with a sixth KC-390 and ten purchase options. As noted by General João Cartaxo Alves:

The acquisition of the sixth KC-390 significantly enhances the strategic and operational capabilities of the Portuguese Air Force by substantially increasing the availability of tactical and logistical transport.

Panama, for its part, signed the purchase of four A-29 Super Tucano to strengthen its surveillance and security capabilities, while Sweden allocated funds in its 2026 budget to advance with the purchase of four C-390 within a joint scheme with the Netherlands and Austria. These three movements allow us to confirm that the ERJ line of defense is not a future bet but is already something tangible that expands in Europe and Latin America.

On the other hand, in its report, ERJ also highlighted that tariffs of 10% in the U.S. did not affect the quarter, reiterating its guidance for this year: 77-85 commercial, 145-155 executives, revenues between $7.0-7.5B, adjusted EBIT margin of 7.5-8.3%, and FCF of at least $200M. I believe that meeting these goals will be the key to consolidating the perception of stability that Fitch incorporated in its positive review.

Q2 2025

In financial terms, during Q2 2025, ERJ revenues were $1.8B (+22% YoY), with adjusted EBIT of $180M, resulting in a margin of 9.9%, and an EBITDA of $233M, with a margin of 12.8%. This shows that the improvement in margins compared to 2024 is already beginning to be reflected in the operating structure.

On the other hand, deliveries of 61 aircraft show a rise of 29.8% YoY (vs. 47 in Q2 2024), which leads us to validate the diversification strategy, since while executives, the core of ERJ, represented more than half, the commercial and defense segments also accounted for an important part of the growth.

Adjusted ex-Eve FCF closed negative at $162M, down from $56M in Q2 2024. The explanation given by ERJ in its official report is the accumulation of inventories and contract assets in preparation for a more loaded H2 of deliveries. Consolidated cash was $1.6B, and although the ex-Eve net position remained negative (-$688.7M), liquidity remains robust.

The implicit message I see in these results is that the backlog is transforming into volume and margins, but we still do not see it reflected in box. I think this is the challenge, and that conversion will be the critical point for the next two quarters. If the strike in Brazil is prolonged, which I will discuss later in more detail, it may complicate precisely this transition.

Valuation

In analyzing ERJ’s valuation, I note that it reflects a point of tension between what it has already achieved and what it still needs to demonstrate.

In multiples, the stock trades with a P/E FWD of 27.5x and an EV/EBITDA FWD of 14.0x, both above the sector median, while the EV/EBIT FWD of 19.4x is also located in demanding terrain. In addition, the PEG at 2.25 suggests that the market is already discounting much of the expected growth, although metrics such as the P/CF FWD at 8.4x offer us a more attractive face against its peers.

However, I believe that the attractiveness lies in the growth of ERJ since, according to consensus, revenues would increase by 17%, while the projected rise in EPS is 48%, which shows a much more aggressive growth profile than the industry average. This explains why the market is willing to pay a premium: it is assumed that normalization of margins and compliance with the guide will transform the record backlog into visible results.

The other side of the coin is profitability. At the operational level, ERJ closed Q2 2025 with an adjusted EBIT margin of 9.9% and an EBITDA margin of 12.8%, figures that represent an improvement over previous years but are still below the industry leaders. While the net return remained at 5.4%, also below the median of the sector (6.1%). What do these numbers tell us? That while ERJ is capturing growth, it still needs to consolidate margins to justify the current multiples.

Risks

Today, I have no doubt that the main risk is execution. Embraer has a record backlog, expanding contracts, and real diversification, but it must demonstrate that it can transform that portfolio into positive cash flow. The strike that began on 17 September in Brazil serves as a reminder of how fragile the production chain can be. According to Reuters:

Metalworkers at Brazilian planemaker Embraer went on strike on Wednesday for an indefinite time, demanding wage increases and the signing of a collective labor agreement"

If the conflict continues, there is a risk that H2 2025 will fail to capture the planned volume.

At the financial level, working capital remains a focus. The accumulation of inventories in H1 2025 must be reversed to reach the FCF target that ERJ sets in its guide.

At the external level, U.S. tariffs still represent a risk, albeit limited, and competition from Boeing and Airbus remains structural in any scenario. Finally, as in all Latin American countries, exposure to the Brazilian real adds FX (BRL:USD) volatility

Conclusion

ERJ keeps intact its structural thesis, which I have been supporting for several quarters: a manufacturer with a solid reputation in regional growth in executives and a line of defense that gains prominence in Europe and Latin America.

Q2 2025 validated this narrative with record numbers in backlog and revenue, and announcements from Portugal, Panama, and Sweden reinforce the traction in defense. However, when we analyzed the valuation, we saw that the stock was already discounting much of that history, with demanding multiples and a cash flow that does not yet show the expected solidity. The strike in Brazil, meanwhile, introduces an additional short-term risk that forces us to be cautious.

For all this, I maintain my ERJ stance with a Hold rating. The long term is still promising, but I prefer to wait for confirmations on margins, cash, and execution before considering a more aggressive position.

data.title: National Storage Affiliates: Fed Rate Cuts May Bring Long-Awaited Turnaround
data.description:
Introduction

So far in 2025, National Storage Affiliates (NYSE:NSA) has significantly underperformed the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ), delivering a loss of ~15%:

This poor performance is in line with the trend observed since 2022, with NSA affected by both higher interest rates as well as a challenging operating environment in the storage market. I last covered the company in February 2025, ranking the common shares a Hold due to the uncertain recovery in net operating income (NOI) growth.

At the same time, I attributed a Buy rating to the Series A (NYSE:NSA.PR.A) and Series B (NYSE:NSA.PR.B) 6% fixed-rate preferred shares, highlighting their well-covered dividends and capital gain potential.

Since then the company has fallen short of my expectations, cutting its Core FFO outlook to just $2.17-2.23/share in 2025, well below my $2.55/share outlook, which incorporated 1% NOI growth this year.

While Fed rate cuts may finally bring a turnaround in NSA's operating performance (given the company's elevated sensitivity to housing activity), for the time being, I maintain my Hold rating on the common shares, as the market-implied cap rate of only ~6.1% does not reflect the current pressure on NOI. If anything, it is still indicative of a turnaround starting in the near-term future.

The company's Series A & Series B preferred shares remain attractively valued, offering a well-covered current yield marginally above 6%, as well as some capital gain potential, which I expect to be realized by late 2026/early 2027.

Company Overview

You can access all NSA results on its website here. National Storage Affiliates is a self-storage REIT focused on the Sunbelt region, which accounts for 66% of the company's store count:

The company's enterprise value amounts to ~$7.9 billion, of which 53% is funded with common equity, 4% with preferred shares, leaving 43% for net debt.

Q2 2025 Results Overview

NSA reported a Core FFO of $0.55/share, down 11.3% Y/Y. Results were impacted by marginally weaker revenues (-0.8% Y/Y), higher property operating expenses (+6.6% Y/Y), as well as higher interest costs.

While general and administrative expenses did decline 21% Y/Y (thanks to optimization efforts), this was more than offset by a 17% increase in shares outstanding, particularly operating partnership units:

As the company is growing in size (on an absolute basis Core FFO was 4.4% higher Y/Y at $74.38 million), it makes sense to look at the company's same-store operating performance.

From the snippet below, we observe that NOI declined 6.1% Y/Y, impacted by weaker revenues and rising expenses, as well as a 2.4% decrease in occupancy to 84.2%:

Overall I think it is fair to say that the turnaround in operational performance is taking longer than anticipated. The silver lining is that as long as occupancy largely stabilizes around current levels, we should see a smaller decline in NOI going forward, with an eventual return to NOI growth in the years ahead.

Reduced 2025 Guidance

Against the backdrop of underwhelming operating results, NSA reduced its Core FFO outlook to $2.17-2.23/share in 2025, down from $2.30-2.38/share previously:

I should note that the revised Core FFO outlook is well below my initial $2.55/share estimate for 2025, which envisaged 1% in NOI growth this year. Indeed, NOI growth remains elusive for NSA, although with Fed rate cuts back in play, I expect a more dynamic housing market in 2026-2027, which bodes well for storage REITs as they benefit from increased demand from people moving in and out of properties.

Fading interest rate headwinds

NSA currently has 12% exposure to floating-rate debt, which carries an interest rate of 5.84% as of H1 2025 (spread of 1.55% over the daily secured overnight financing rate ("SOFR")).

With the Fed signaling a Fed funds rate of about 3.6% at the end of 2025, we observe that variable interest rate costs should decrease to about 5.09% at the end of this year. This in turn should decrease 2026 annual interest costs by $3 million, or a boost to Core FFO of $0.022/share.

The boost from lower rates may be partially offset by refinancing of fixed-rate debt, with 2026 maturities carrying an interest rate of 4.33%. Still, with markets expecting a Fed funds rate of about 3% at the end of 2026, odds are that NSA manages to refinance its expiring maturities at rates only slightly above the current 4.33%.

All in all, the company's 4.52% weighted average cost of debt is likely to remain little changed over the long term. While NSA will enjoy a short-term boost to Core FFO next year, refinancing legacy fixed-rate maturities in 2026 and beyond (some of which carry interest rates below market interest rates) should offset this effect over the medium term:

NSA Valuation

The shares currently trade at 14x the revised Core FFO midpoint of $2.20/share. In itself, this is quite attractive when you take into account the conservative capital structure.

However the million-dollar question is whether Core FFO returns to growth in 2026. The company will indeed enjoy a small marginal boost to Core FFO from lower interest costs. Looking at current rates of decline in net operating income, it is hard to expect a sharp turnaround in 2026. Still, I am somewhat optimistic that NOI dynamics are close to a bottom, with a stabilization in the housing market driving a pickup in demand in 2026-2027.

This may explain why the company opted not to cut its $0.57/share quarterly dividend in August. Indeed, its current annual dividend run-rate of $2.28/share is not covered by Core FFO. This was also discussed on the conference call, although exact details were scant:

Yes, good question. We certainly know that we're at a higher payout ratio than we've ever been, and we're currently above the payout that we're earning. And I would tell you, our Board is very thoughtful as we are as a leadership team on how we evaluate the dividend policy and the payout ratio. We routinely discuss the current state of our business as well as the near and long-term outlooks. Our Board has insights to our initiatives, our strategies, what the company is deploying. They have very deep knowledge of the self- storage sector and the dynamics of the self-storage sector.

I would tell you, I think the Board understands the long-term plan as well as the impact of the cycles of the sector. So I think it also plays into our ability to really make a meaningful improvement in a relatively short time frame because of the short contract rates that we have, the short month leases we have. I think our Board has a long-term view and has a good understanding of where we're at and where we're headed.

With 47% of enterprise value funded with net debt and preferred shares, I think it makes sense to look at cumulative cash flows to enterprise value. The company generated $121 million in Q2 2025 net operating income, implying a current run-rate close to $484 million. Relative to the $7.9 billion enterprise value, this represents a market-implied cap rate of 6.1%.

This would be quite attractive as long as you are on board with the idea of NOI stabilizing in 2026 before returning to growth in 2027. For the time being, however, I still believe that a Hold rating is appropriate for NSA. The truth is current NOI dynamics put the REIT in the same boat as distressed office REITs, which trade at bargain valuations (cap rates between 8-12%). As such, it is hard to get excited about a 6.1% cap rate with uncertain growth prospects.

The preferred shares

In contrast to NSA's struggling common stock, the company's preferred shares have done quite well so far in 2025, delivering gains of ~10-16%, ahead of the ~7% gain for the iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF (PFF):

Preferred dividends remain safe and well-covered. The quarterly $5.1 million cumulative preferred dividend accounts for just 6.4% of Q2 2025 Core FFO before preferred distributions. Both issues trade marginally below par value, with preferred equity covered by common equity some 13x.

In the table below, I have compiled key data for the company's Series A & Series B preferred shares:

Series Coupon Current Yield Discount to par
A 6% 6.36% 5.3%
B 6% 6.71% 10.8%

Source: Author calculations

As explained in the comments section in a previous article on the preferred shares here, the larger yield on the Series B shares somewhat compensates for the risk of the common shares falling below $20.21/share in the event of a takeover. In that case, preferred shareholders may get less than par value for their shares. While this scenario is quite remote in my opinion (I do not see the common shares falling 30% from current levels), the Series A shares offer greater protection as the trigger for conversion there stands at $12.215/share.

With that disclaimer out of the way, I believe both the Series A and Series B 6% fixed-rate preferred shares are still worth a Buy rating. I think that both issues will trade close to par value in late 2026/early 2027, providing investors with an attractive current yield and a small capital gain.

Indeed, if NSA's long-term cost of debt remains around 4.5%, I think the company will have little problem issuing preferred shares with a coupon of about 5.5% once the Fed is done normalizing its monetary policy. This trend is already happening in the Eurozone (where interest rates are close to neutral levels), with Unibail-Rodamco-Westfield (OTCPK:UNBLF) issuing new hybrid notes with a circa 0.4% lower coupon than that of the previous hybrid issue.

Risks

The main risk in the common stock investment case remains a further decline in operating momentum. Indeed, while I do expect the company's Core FFO to begin to stabilize in 2026 before returning to growth in 2027, this is far from certain.

Interest expenses may continue to rise if the Fed does not continue with interest rate cuts. Case in point, we observe that the Central Bank's September 2025 summary of economic projections shows a Fed funds rate of 3.4% at the end of 2026, while markets price in a rate closer to 3%:

All else equal, I expect the outlook for Fed rate cuts to shift favorably in June 2026, when it will reflect the new Fed chairman's influence on discussions.

Last but not least, a potential dividend reduction may have a short-term negative impact on the common share price, although I would view it as a buying opportunity.

Conclusion

NSA reported weaker Core FFO per share in Q2 2025, impacted by a challenging operating environment, higher interest costs, and a rising share count.

Looking ahead, the company will enjoy a near-term boost from Fed rate cuts, while a pickup in housing activity may bring a long-awaited improvement in occupancy. Indeed, NSA reports that 57% housing is owner-occupied in its markets:

Against this backdrop, I still believe that the company's preferred shares offer a better risk-reward ratio, confirming my Buy rating. I maintain my Hold rating on the common shares as the NOI turnaround in 2026-2027 is far from certain.

Thank you for reading.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Amazon Shares Still Have Room To Grow On Supply Chain Improvements
data.description:
Amazon.com, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMZN) has contrasting forces at play that may be holding back shares from stronger growth. With growing concerns for consumer spending as the U.S. economy weakens, growth across its consumer-facing businesses may be at risk. On the other hand, Amazon has a growing backlog for cloud computing and is actively improving its operating margins by incrementally improving its supply chain while automating more warehouse operations. With the appealing outlook for AWS growth, I am reiterating my "Buy" rating for Amazon shares with a price target of $252/share at 16.19x eFY26 EV/EBITDA.

Amazon Operations

Amazon has been making substantial improvements to its fulfillment supply chain, improving speed and efficiency by regionalizing facilities. This resulted in a 40% improvement from fulfillment to delivery without extra stops in Q2’25 while reducing the average distance traveled by 12%.

In addition to improving the supply chain, Amazon is focusing on improving its cost structure through automation and robotics with over 1 million robots deployed across its global fulfillment network. Autonomous operations include package sorting and movement across fulfillment facilities with mobile robots. As part of this, Amazon rolled out DeepFleet, its AI foundational model for robot orchestration. Considering the rapid shift towards autonomous warehouse operations, this combination of hardware and software may bring Amazon closer to creating a fully autonomous fulfillment operation.

As part of its autonomous initiative, Amazon is taking aim at Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL) and Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) with Zoox robotaxis. In early August 2025, Zoox received a demonstration exemption from federal safety requirements, allowing the vehicle to be used for demonstrations and testing without manual driving controls. On Sept. 10, 2025, Zoox officially launched its autonomous vehicle service around the Las Vegas Strip, making it the first purpose-built autonomous ride-hailing vehicle robotaxi, far ahead of Tesla’s planned 2026 Cybercab release. Similar to other ride-hailing applications like Uber Technologies, Inc. (UBER) or Lyft, Inc. (LYFT), users can call for a ride through its application for service to predetermined destinations, with more to come. Though this may be a hit to Tesla’s ambitious goal of being the go-to robotaxi on the market, Zoox doesn’t appear to use general AI features that Tesla is aiming to lean on, potentially limiting its full capabilities in the near term. Given the scale of the project, I do not anticipate Zoox to be accretive to earnings for quite some time. Considering the grand scheme of things, I believe Zoox could potentially usher in the next era of fulfillment operations for Amazon, potentially taking over final-mile delivery in due time.

As for Amazon’s Advertising business, Amazon announced a partnership with Roku, Inc. (ROKU) in June 2025, broadening its CTV advertisement footprint. The partnership is expected to broaden Amazon and Roku’s advertising reach across major streaming applications, allowing advertisers to plan campaigns across both platforms. I believe this partnership can substantially improve ad targeting, particularly when considering Roku TV users streaming Prime Video. Amazon struck a similar deal with Disney in June 2025, broadening Amazon advertisers’ reach to Disney+, Hulu, and ESPN, as well as Netflix on Sept. 10, 2025.

In Amazon Web Services [AWS], Amazon has been deepening its partnership with Anthropic, hosting its AI models using Trainium2. On Sept. 4, 2025, Seeking Alpha published that AWS is building three data center campuses for Anthropic for 1.3GW of capacity. Accordingly, the data center sites are expected to host 1 million Trainium2 chips as one of the largest clusters of non-NVIDIA chips. According to the SemiAnalysis report quoted, Tranium2 chips are expected to provide a substantially lower total cost of ownership [TCO] relative to an Nvidia GB200 NVL70 cluster, potentially improving the economics behind Anthropic’s models. This could be a major revenue driver for Amazon as a cloud host and chip designer. This data center build-out can also benefit Marvell Technology, Inc. (MRVL) as a primary chip manufacturing partner for Trainium2.

As for cloud hosting, management indicated in Q2’25 that customers are resuming workload migration to AWS sites. In total, Amazon ended the quarter with $195 billion in backlog, suggesting significant demand for AWS cloud services. Looking ahead, I’m forecasting AWS revenue to continue to scale, accelerating into the back half of eFY25.

Going back to Amazon stores, I’m expecting some headwinds in the coming quarters as consumer confidence weakens. Accordingly, consumers are facing inflationary pressures and a heightened risk of job loss that may lead to lower discretionary spending. The most impacted consumer may be Gen Z; a report published by Yahoo! Finance has suggested that Gen Z is skipping meals, delaying medical care, selling belongings, and dipping into retirement savings to cover the cost of rent. This may be indicative of what’s to come for Amazon.

Making matters worse, CNBC recently reported that small businesses are expecting to increase prices to offset tariff costs. Accordingly, many of the price increases were delayed following the order pull-forward going into the tariff environment. Given the recent U.S. inflation print of 2.9%, I suspect inflationary pressures may mount in the coming months as price increases deepen into overall costs.

Amazon Financial Position

Looking ahead to eQ3’25, I’m forecasting Amazon to generate $175 billion in net revenue with a diluted EPS of $1.57/share. Operations will in part be driven by improved operational efficiencies as Amazon invests in automating more of the supply chain while enhancing regional distribution. Amazon closed Q2’25 with $93 billion in cash & equivalents and marketable securities and $50.7 billion in debt on the balance sheet for a net cash position of $42.5 billion.

Risks Related to Amazon

Bull Case

Amazon is improving operational efficiencies by automating more of the supply chain, leaning into robotics and automation to improve fulfillment. AWS continues to be a major growth driver as Amazon invests in building out capacity to support its growing backlog. As part of its partnership with Anthropic, Amazon should realize significant growth for AWS, particularly as it leverages its Trainium2 chip design.

Bear Case

Amazon may face certain headwinds for its consumer-facing businesses as consumers face mounting inflationary and tariff pressure. Though I anticipate a portion of the costs to be offset by automating operations, the top line may suffer some pull if consumer spending weakens.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

AMZN shares currently trade at 18.26x EV/EBITDA, roughly the midpoint of shares’ historical premium range.

AMZN shares currently trade at a discount to its peers, likely the result of concerns for its consumer-facing businesses. This could potentially create some opportunity for a certain degree of mean remediation to match its hyperscaler peer premium.

Valuing AMZN shares, I believe shares should trade at $252/share at 16.19x eFY26 EV/EBITDA; I am recommending AMZN shares with a "Buy" rating.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market cap and relative multiple.
